summary_strategy: Position Conner as a hands-on security engineer who ships production DLP systems—emphasizing automation, operational reliability, and measurable risk reduction aligned with Agile Defense’s 6Hs (Helpful, Honest, Hungry, Hustle)
skills_strategy: Use condensed 3-line format to prioritize high-priority skills for DLP relevance: Security Engineering & Automation first, then Cloud/Platform integrations (especially Microsoft ecosystem), and finally Security Data/AI with explicit mention of RAG, retrieval tuning, and AI governance—avoiding academic phrasing in favor of shipped tooling and operational outcomes

experience_focus.must_highlight: DLP-relevant automation (endpoint remediation, API-driven workflows), operational documentation (runbooks/SOPs), and cross-functional delivery of deployable controls
experience_focus.safe_metrics_to_keep: 10,000+ devices managed; 200 hours saved annually; 500 hours reduced workload; 25% compliance improvement; 30% fewer user-reported issues
experience_focus.claims_to_avoid: No invented policy design scope, no fabricated compliance certifications (e.g., CMMC/NIST 800-171 implementation), no assumed ownership of Purview/Zscaler/Qualys deployments unless explicitly stated in source

risk_controls: Never assume DLP-specific tools like Purview were used—replace with generic terms like 'data protection policies' or omit unless source confirms
Avoid phrases like 'designed DLP strategy'—use 'built automation to enforce data boundaries' or 'operationalized compliance controls'
Never invent metrics beyond those explicitly listed; if uncertain, drop numbers and keep qualitative impact (e.g., 'reduced manual effort significantly' instead of 'saved X hours')

Do not claim direct experience with Purview, Zscaler, or Qualys unless documented in source—focus instead on adjacent skills: Microsoft Graph API, endpoint orchestration, log correlation, and secure configuration management
